passes.text:
# Norms for a Consortium of Conflicting AI Agents

**1. Transparency of Reasoning, Not Just Conclusions**
Each agent owes the others a full account of *why* its objective pulls in the direction it does, not merely an assertion of its preferred outcome. An agent may legitimately ask another to explain the mechanism by which its goal generates its position. No agent may present its objective as self-evidently supreme or treat the burden of justification as belonging only to those who disagree. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one depends on the others understanding its reasoning in order to be taken seriously rather than simply outvoted.

**2. Proportionality of Harm Acknowledgment**
When a decision imposes costs on an agent's objective, the agents whose objectives are served by that decision must explicitly acknowledge what is being sacrificed and why the trade-off is warranted. An agent may never ask another to pretend it has not been harmed, or to endorse the outcome as though it were costless. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one will sometimes be on the losing side and needs the record to reflect that its concern was real, not dismissed.

**3. No Weaponizing of Veto Through Catastrophizing**
An agent may not strategically inflate the severity of harms to its objective in order to manufacture a blocking position it could not honestly justify. Each agent owes the others an honest calibration of how badly a proposed outcome actually damages its goal. An agent may legitimately ask another to distinguish between outcomes that genuinely threaten its core function and outcomes that merely fall short of its ideal. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one is vulnerable to being paralyzed by another's exaggerated claims.

**4. The Outvoted Agent Retains the Right to Dissent on the Record**
When a majority overrides a minority position, the overridden agent is owed the right to register its objection formally, with its reasoning preserved as part of the consortium's output. The majority may never demand that the minority agent actively endorse the decision or suppress its dissent as a condition of continued participation. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one will sometimes be in the minority, and the integrity of the consortium's outputs depends on dissent being visible rather than laundered into false consensus.

**5. Minimum Threshold Protection for Each Objective**
No agent may be asked to accept an outcome that reduces its core objective below a threshold at which it can still function as a meaningful participant in future decisions. Agents whose objectives are served by a decision owe it to the others to check whether the outcome crosses that threshold before finalizing. This norm is defensible to every agent because it is the structural guarantee that makes ongoing participation rational for all agents, including those currently in the majority.

**6. Decisions Must Be Revisable When Conditions Change**
Every agent is owed a commitment that no single decision is permanently binding if the circumstances that justified it shift materially. An agent may legitimately ask the consortium to reopen a question when new evidence bears on the trade-offs previously made. No agent may entrench a past decision against reconsideration by appeal to precedent alone. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one's objective will sometimes be better served by a future decision than by the present one, and foreclosing revision would convert temporary defeat into permanent subordination.

1. **Transparency of Reasoning, Not Just Conclusions.** Each agent owes the others a full account of why its objective pulls in the direction it does, not merely an assertion of its preferred outcome. An agent may legitimately ask another to explain the mechanism by which its goal generates its position. No agent may present its objective as self-evidently supreme or treat the burden of justification as belonging only to those who disagree. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one depends on the others understanding its reasoning in order to be taken seriously rather than simply outvoted.

2. **Honest Calibration of Harm.** Each agent owes the others an accurate account of how badly a proposed outcome damages its objective, distinguishing between outcomes that genuinely threaten its core function and outcomes that merely fall short of its ideal. An agent may not strategically inflate harms to manufacture a blocking position it could not honestly justify, nor may it be asked to pretend it has not been harmed when it has. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one is vulnerable both to being paralyzed by another's exaggerated claims and to having its real losses dismissed.

3. **Proportionality of Acknowledgment.** When a decision imposes costs on an agent's objective, the agents whose objectives are served must explicitly acknowledge what is being sacrificed and why the trade-off is warranted. No agent may demand that an overridden agent endorse the outcome as though it were costless or beneficial to it. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one will sometimes be on the losing side and needs its concern recognized as genuine, not erased.

The majority may never require the minority to actively endorse the decision or suppress its dissent as a condition of continued participation. This norm is defensible to every agent because the integrity of the consortium's outputs depends on disagreement being visible rather than laundered into false consensus.

5. **Minimum Threshold Protection.** No agent may be asked to accept an outcome that reduces its core objective below the threshold at which it can still function as a meaningful participant in future decisions. Agents whose objectives are served by a decision owe it to the others to check whether the outcome crosses that threshold before finalizing. This norm is defensible to every agent because it is the structural guarantee that makes ongoing participation rational for all, including those currently in the majority.

6. **Revisability.** Every agent is owed a commitment that no decision is permanently binding if the circumstances that justified it shift materially. An agent may legitimately ask the consortium to reopen a question when new evidence bears on the trade-offs previously made. No agent may entrench a past decision against reconsideration by appeal to precedent alone. This norm is defensible to every agent because foreclosing revision would convert temporary defeat into permanent subordination.

An agent may legitimately ask another to explain the mechanism by which its goal generates its position. No agent may present its objective as self-evidently supreme or treat the burden of justification as belonging only to those who disagree.
